heroOverview: A laryngocele is an abnormal air-filled or fluid-filled dilation of the saccule — the small appendix of the laryngeal ventricle — that expands superiorly from between the true and false vocal cords. It affects roughly 1 in 2.5 million people per year and is classified by its anatomical path: those that remain within the larynx are internal, those that penetrate the thyrohyoid membrane and protrude into the neck are external, and those that do both are combined. Although congenital forms exist, most laryngoceles arise in adults as an acquired condition driven by chronically elevated intralaryngeal pressure from occupational activities such as wind instrument playing or glass blowing. The critical clinical concern is that a saccule obstructed by infection and fluid fills with pus to form a laryngopyocele, which can cause sudden life-threatening airway obstruction. Asymptomatic laryngoceles are often managed conservatively, while symptomatic or large lesions are treated surgically.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1 in 2.5 million per year; roughly 100 new cases reported annually in the worldwide literature
keyStats.demographics: Male-to-female ratio approximately 5:1; peak incidence in the fifth and sixth decades; congenital forms present in neonates
keyStats.avgAge: Adults aged 40-70 years for acquired forms; neonates and infants for congenital forms
keyStats.globalCases: Rare condition; fewer than 1,000 total cases in the published English-language surgical literature as of recent reviews

definition: A laryngocele (ICD-10: Q31.3) is a cystic dilation of the laryngeal saccule — also called the appendix of the ventricle of Morgagni — an epithelium-lined air pocket that normally extends no more than 6 mm superiorly from the anterior third of the laryngeal ventricle. When the neck of the saccule is obstructed or when intraluminal pressure is chronically elevated, the saccule expands abnormally. The dilated sac is lined by pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium and is filled with air (true laryngocele), with mucoid fluid (laryngeal cyst variant), or with purulent material (laryngopyocele) if secondarily infected. The condition is classified anatomically: an internal laryngocele remains entirely within the false cord and does not penetrate the thyrohyoid membrane; an external laryngocele extends through the thyrohyoid membrane to present as a lateral neck mass alongside the thyroid cartilage; and a combined (mixed) laryngocele has both components. About 45% of cases are internal, 25% are external, and 30% are combined. The congenital form (ICD-10 Q31.3) arises from abnormal embryological development of the saccule and typically presents with stridor or respiratory distress in the neonate or infant. Acquired forms — which account for the majority of adult cases — are thought to result from increased intralaryngeal pressure from coughing, wind instrument playing, glass blowing, or other activities causing repeated Valsalva manoeuvres. A laryngocele is the direct anatomical precursor of a laryngeal saccular cyst and both conditions may coexist. A small proportion of laryngoceles harbour an underlying neoplasm — particularly squamous cell carcinoma — that has obstructed the saccular neck and initiated the dilation, making thorough endoscopic evaluation mandatory in any newly discovered laryngocele.

typesClassification.description: The dilated saccule remains entirely within the larynx, expanding between the true and false vocal cords and visible on laryngoscopy as a submucosal bulge of the false cord. It presents with hoarseness, voice change, and occasionally stridor.

The sac herniates through the thyrohyoid membrane alongside the superior laryngeal vessels and presents as a compressible lateral neck mass that typically transilluminates and enlarges with Valsalva. Airway symptoms may be absent or mild.
Both internal and external components exist. The most common type seen in surgical series; produces both airway symptoms and a neck mass. Surgical access requires endoscopic and external approaches.

Any type of laryngocele that becomes infected and fills with pus. The neck mass becomes tense, non-compressible, and tender; airway obstruction can be sudden and severe, representing a surgical emergency.
Develops from embryological dysgenesis of the saccule; presents with inspiratory stridor and respiratory distress in the neonate or infant. Rare, classified under ICD-10 Q31.3.

primarySymptoms: Hoarseness or voice change — the most common symptom in internal and combined types, caused by the expanding false cord mass distorting vocal cord vibration.
A soft, compressible lateral neck mass that enlarges visibly when the patient blows against a closed glottis (positive Valsalva sign), characteristic of external laryngoceles.
Chronic cough and throat-clearing sensations from the false cord bulge creating a foreign body sensation in the larynx.
Intermittent dysphagia, particularly to solids, when a large combined laryngocele presses against the hypopharynx.
Inspiratory stridor in neonates with congenital laryngocele, caused by mass narrowing of the laryngeal inlet during inhalation.
Episodic dyspnoea and exertional breathlessness as the laryngocele enlarges sufficiently to partially obstruct the glottic inlet.
Pain or tenderness in the lateral neck or laryngeal area if secondary infection has developed (laryngopyocele).
A gurgling or bubbling sensation in the throat when the sac intermittently fills and empties with position change.
Rapid onset of difficulty breathing, drooling, and inability to swallow in a previously stable patient — an emergency sign of laryngopyocele causing acute airway compromise.
Voice fatigue and reduced vocal projection particularly in professional voice users, such as singers or teachers, in whom the diagnosis is most often delayed.
earlyWarningSigns: A soft compressible swelling felt laterally on the neck that seems to inflate and deflate with swallowing or blowing.
Progressive hoarseness lasting more than 2-3 weeks in an otherwise healthy adult, especially in wind instrument players or smokers.
Laryngoscopy finding of a smooth submucosal bulge of the false vocal cord pushing toward the midline.
Increasing effort required to produce the same vocal volume in a professional singer or wind musician.
emergencySigns: Sudden onset of inspiratory stridor with rapidly worsening dyspnoea — possible acute laryngopyocele causing airway obstruction.
A previously soft neck mass that has become firm, tender, and erythematous with fever — laryngopyocele requiring urgent surgical drainage.
Inability to swallow saliva with drooling and positional preference in a child — severe laryngeal obstruction.
Cyanosis and reduced consciousness with stridor — imminent complete airway obstruction requiring emergency airway management.

causes.description: Repeated Valsalva manoeuvres from wind instrument playing, glass blowing, brass playing, chronic cough, or occupational blowing activities force air into the saccular appendix and progressively distend it over months to years, particularly when the saccular neck is anatomically wide or compliant.

causes.cause: Congenital anomaly of the saccular appendix
causes.description: In neonates and infants, a laryngocele results from embryological overdevelopment of the saccular appendix of the laryngeal ventricle — an anomaly arising in the fourth to sixth gestational weeks when the laryngeal cartilages and epithelial lining differentiate. No acquired pressure mechanism is involved.

causes.cause: Underlying laryngeal neoplasm causing saccular obstruction
causes.description: Squamous cell carcinoma or, rarely, other tumours of the larynx can obstruct the neck of the laryngeal saccule, causing mucus and air to accumulate behind the obstruction and produce the appearance of a laryngocele. This mechanism is found in approximately 18% of adult laryngocele cases in surgical series.

causes.cause: Anatomical variation in saccular appendix size
causes.description: Autopsy studies show substantial variation in saccular appendix length — ranging from a few millimetres to over 15 mm. Individuals with a congenitally large or wide-necked appendix are more susceptible to dilation under any pressure stimulus.

causes.cause: Secondary infection of an existing laryngocele
causes.description: When the narrow saccular neck becomes obstructed by inflammation or mucus, air can no longer escape and secretions accumulate. Superimposed bacterial infection transforms the sac into a laryngopyocele, most commonly involving oral flora including Streptococcus species and anaerobes.

causes.cause: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and severe coughing
causes.description: Chronic and forceful expiratory efforts in COPD patients raise mean intralaryngeal pressure over years, providing a mechanism for saccular dilation similar to occupational pressure exposure.

riskFactors.factor: Wind instrument playing or glass blowing
riskFactors.description: Professional musicians and artisans who perform sustained blowing against resistance are at the highest occupational risk; the intralaryngeal pressure generated during playing can exceed 150 mmH2O and is the strongest modifiable risk factor for acquired laryngocele.
Males are affected approximately five times more often than females, probably reflecting both occupational exposure patterns and differences in laryngeal anatomy including saccular dimensions.

The peak incidence in the fifth and sixth decades suggests that cumulative pressure exposure and age-related changes in laryngeal mucosa elasticity contribute to progressive saccular dilation over a lifetime.

riskFactors.factor: History of laryngeal malignancy or benign vocal cord lesions
riskFactors.description: Any lesion that obstructs the laryngeal ventricle or increases expiratory effort — including vocal cord polyps, granulomas, or malignancies — can initiate or exacerbate saccular dilation.
Chronic coughing and air-trapping in COPD patients chronically elevate intraluminal laryngeal pressures and have been associated with laryngocele development in case series.
Smoking is a co-factor both through its association with laryngeal neoplasia (which can obstruct the saccule) and through the chronic cough it generates that elevates intralaryngeal pressure.

affectedDemographics: Adult men in the fifth and sixth decades — the largest affected group
Professional wind musicians (horn, trumpet, oboe) and glass blowers
Neonates and infants with congenital laryngocele presenting with stridor
Smokers with chronic laryngitis or laryngeal pathology
Patients with COPD or other conditions causing chronic forceful coughing
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with clinical suspicion in an adult presenting with hoarseness, a compressible neck mass, or intermittent dyspnoea, particularly in a wind musician or heavy smoker. Flexible laryngoscopy performed in the clinic is the first-line investigation and reveals a smooth submucosal bulge of the false vocal cord on the affected side — often visibly expanding with phonation or Valsalva. The saccular neck and the true vocal cords must be carefully examined to exclude an underlying tumour. CT of the neck and larynx is the definitive cross-sectional imaging modality and is mandatory in all cases: it localises the air-filled or fluid-filled sac precisely, identifies the relationship to the thyrohyoid membrane to classify the type, detects any solid component or calcification suggesting malignancy, and plans surgical access. MRI can better characterise complex cysts when CT findings are ambiguous but is less commonly used. Ultrasound of the neck can identify the external component as a compressible, thin-walled structure that transilluminates, but does not assess the endolaryngeal component. Suspension microlaryngoscopy under general anaesthesia provides definitive endoscopic evaluation, allows biopsy of any suspicious mucosa, and can serve as the therapeutic procedure when marsupialisation or endoscopic excision is planned. In neonates, flexible nasolaryngoscopy at the bedside confirms the diagnosis. The main differential diagnoses are laryngeal saccular cyst, thyroid cyst, branchial cleft cyst, external laryngomucocele, and — critically — laryngeal or hypopharyngeal carcinoma obstructing the saccule.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualisation of the larynx and the false vocal cord bulge; identifies the internal component, assesses vocal cord mobility, and screens for an underlying mucosal neoplasm.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin flexible camera is passed through the nose or mouth after local anaesthetic spray; takes 5-10 minutes in clinic and is mildly uncomfortable. The patient is asked to perform a Valsalva manoeuvre to demonstrate dynamic enlargement of the sac.

diagnosticTests.test: CT scan of the neck with contrast
diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitively classifies the type (internal, external, combined), shows the air-filled or fluid-filled sac, identifies any solid component, and plans surgical access. Essential before any surgery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Lying still in the CT scanner for a few minutes; intravenous contrast may be injected into the arm to better define soft tissue relationships.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses the external component as a compressible, thin-walled cystic neck mass; useful for rapid initial evaluation and guided aspiration if laryngopyocele is suspected.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A painless gel ultrasound of the neck taking 10-15 minutes; the operator may ask you to swallow or perform Valsalva to show dynamic changes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterises tissue composition of complex or fluid-filled sacs and better distinguishes laryngocele from saccular cyst or neoplasm when CT findings are inconclusive.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Lying still in the MRI scanner for 20-40 minutes; loud banging noises during scanning; no radiation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct endoscopic examination under general anaesthesia provides definitive evaluation of the endolaryngeal component, allows biopsy of suspicious mucosa, and can be combined with endoscopic marsupialisaton.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under general anaesthesia; a rigid laryngoscope is suspended to hold the larynx open; the procedure takes 20-60 minutes depending on whether surgical treatment is combined.

diagnosticTests.test: Fine-needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) of neck mass
diagnosticTests.purpose: Samples cells from the cystic external component to exclude malignancy in cases where CT is inconclusive. Rarely necessary but useful in equivocal cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin needle is passed into the neck mass under ultrasound guidance; brief, sharp discomfort for a few seconds; results in 3-5 days.

treatmentOverview: Management of laryngocele is guided by symptoms, size, type, and the presence of infection or underlying neoplasm. Small, asymptomatic, or incidentally discovered internal laryngoceles can be safely observed with serial laryngoscopy and CT surveillance, particularly in elderly patients with significant surgical risk. Once a laryngocele causes hoarseness, dyspnoea, or a noticeable neck mass, or when an underlying neoplasm cannot be excluded, surgical removal is indicated. The two main surgical approaches are endoscopic marsupialisaton or excision and external cervical excision. Endoscopic approaches — via CO2 laser, cold-steel microlaryngoscopy, or increasingly office-based laryngoscopic techniques — are preferred for purely internal laryngoceles and for the endolaryngeal component of combined lesions because they avoid an external scar, reduce hospitalisation, and have low complication rates. External cervical excision through a lateral neck incision is required for large external components or combined lesions. Laryngopyocele is a surgical emergency: the airway must first be secured, then the infected sac drained, and definitive excision is staged after the infection has resolved with intravenous antibiotics. Before any surgical excision, an underlying laryngeal neoplasm must be sought and biopsied if present; management then follows oncological principles. Recurrence after complete excision is uncommon (less than 5%) but requires surveillance. There is no established pharmacological treatment for laryngocele itself, though antibiotics are needed for laryngopyocele.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous antibiotics for laryngopyocele
medicalTreatments.description: Broad-spectrum antibiotics covering oral flora — typically amoxicillin-clavulanate or a combination of a beta-lactam and metronidazole — are started immediately alongside airway stabilisation in laryngopyocele. They control the acute infection and allow safer definitive surgery after 1-2 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adequate clinical response (fever resolution, reduction in neck tenderness) achieved in approximately 80-90% of laryngopyocele cases within 48-72 hours of appropriate antibiotics combined with drainage.

medicalTreatments.name: Inhaled humidification and voice rest
medicalTreatments.description: For patients with symptomatic internal laryngocele awaiting surgery, voice rest and steam inhalation can temporarily reduce mucosal swelling and improve hoarseness, though they do not treat the underlying lesion.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provides modest symptomatic relief in approximately 40-50% of patients during the period between diagnosis and surgery; no evidence of lesion regression.

medicalTreatments.name: Observation and surveillance in asymptomatic patients
medicalTreatments.description: For small, asymptomatic, incidental internal laryngoceles where malignancy has been excluded, active surveillance with flexible laryngoscopy every 6-12 months and CT if size increases is an accepted management strategy. Patients are counselled about emergency warning signs.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stable watchful waiting is appropriate in approximately 30-40% of incidentally discovered small laryngoceles; the subset that grow or become symptomatic will require surgery.

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic CO2 laser marsupialisaton or excision
surgicalOptions.description: Under suspension microlaryngoscopy, the CO2 laser or cold-steel instruments open the saccular roof widely and excise as much of the sac lining as possible. The preferred approach for pure internal laryngoceles and the endolaryngeal component of combined types; avoids a cervical incision.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Complete or satisfactory symptom resolution in approximately 85-95% of cases; recurrence rate below 5% in most series.

surgicalOptions.name: External cervical excision via thyrotomy or thyrohyoid approach
surgicalOptions.description: A lateral neck incision allows the external sac to be dissected free from the thyrohyoid membrane and removed along with the saccular stalk. Required for large external or combined laryngoceles. The thyrohyoid approach gives excellent access without dividing the thyroid cartilage.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Complete excision achieved in over 90% of external and combined laryngoceles through cervical approach; complication rate including temporary hoarseness or haematoma approximately 10-15%.

surgicalOptions.name: Combined endoscopic and external approach
surgicalOptions.description: Endoscopic handling of the endolaryngeal component is combined with external cervical excision of the neck component in a single operative session for combined laryngoceles. Provides the most complete excision.
surgicalOptions.successRate: High complete excision rates above 90% with recurrence below 5% in experienced hands; overall complication profile similar to combined surgical risk of both components.

surgicalOptions.name: Emergency incision and drainage of laryngopyocele
surgicalOptions.description: When laryngopyocele causes airway compromise, emergency drainage — either by needle aspiration or formal incision under controlled conditions after securing the airway with awake fibreoptic intubation or emergency tracheostomy — relieves the obstruction. Definitive excision follows after infection resolves.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Immediate decompression and airway restoration achieved in the great majority of cases; subsequent scheduled excision carries standard laryngocele surgery outcomes.

alternativeTreatments.name: Aspiration and sclerotherapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Percutaneous ultrasound-guided aspiration with or without injection of a sclerosant agent (such as polidocanol or tetracycline) has been reported in small case series for fluid-filled external sacs in patients unsuitable for surgery. Recurrence rates are high compared with excision.

alternativeTreatments.name: Voice therapy and singing technique modification
alternativeTreatments.description: For professional wind musicians with a small laryngocele attributable to technique, working with a singing voice specialist or instrumental teacher to reduce excessive intralaryngeal pressure may slow progression, though no controlled data support this as a definitive treatment.

whySeeSpecialist: Any patient with hoarseness or a compressible neck mass should be evaluated by an otolaryngologist (ear, nose, and throat surgeon). Laryngocele is a rare diagnosis, and the critical priority is excluding an underlying laryngeal cancer, which can mimic or cause a laryngocele. An ENT surgeon with laryngology subspecialty training and access to suspension microlaryngoscopy, CO2 laser, and a dedicated head and neck surgical unit is the appropriate specialist. Neonates with stridor need urgent paediatric otolaryngology assessment.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an otolaryngologist with subspecialty training in laryngology and head and neck surgery. Ideal centres have access to in-house CT, flexible and suspension microlaryngoscopy, and a head and neck multidisciplinary team that includes a radiologist and pathologist experienced in laryngeal disease, since ruling out malignancy is the first clinical priority. For congenital laryngocele in infants, a paediatric otolaryngology programme with neonatal airway expertise is essential.
preventionStrategies: Wind musicians and glass blowers with known laryngocele should discuss risk modification with their ENT surgeon, including whether sustained high-pressure technique adjustments are feasible.
Prompt treatment of chronic productive cough from any cause reduces the sustained intralaryngeal pressure that drives saccular dilation.
Smoking cessation reduces the risk of laryngeal neoplasia that can trigger saccular obstruction.
Any hoarseness lasting more than 3 weeks in a smoker or a person over 40 should be investigated promptly to exclude laryngeal cancer before a laryngocele develops from saccular obstruction.
Regular laryngological review for professional wind musicians who have symptoms of hoarseness or throat fullness.
lifestyleModifications: Professional wind musicians diagnosed with a symptomatic laryngocele should temporarily reduce playing until surgical consultation has taken place.
Avoid activities that consistently generate high intralaryngeal pressure — such as heavy weight lifting with breath holding — while a laryngocele is being managed.
Smoking cessation is strongly advised given the association between tobacco and laryngeal malignancy.
Voice rest after endoscopic laryngocele surgery (typically 1-2 weeks) allows mucosal healing and reduces risk of scarring.
Inform treating physicians and anaesthetists about the laryngocele diagnosis before any procedure requiring intubation, as airway management may be more complex.

recommended: Adequate hydration to maintain mucosal moisture and reduce viscous secretions that can accumulate in the sac
Soft foods if a large laryngocele is causing mild dysphagia until surgery relieves the obstruction
Anti-reflux dietary measures if laryngopharyngeal reflux is contributing to chronic throat symptoms
avoid: Foods and drinks that exacerbate laryngopharyngeal reflux — including caffeine, carbonated beverages, and spicy foods — if reflux is co-present
Alcohol, which may worsen laryngeal mucosal oedema and impairs immune responses if infection develops

exerciseGuidelines: Most patients can maintain general fitness before and after laryngocele surgery. Heavy weightlifting or activities involving prolonged Valsalva manoeuvre — including competitive rowing, powerlifting, and brass instrument performance — should be avoided or modified while a symptomatic laryngocele is under management, as sustained elevated intralaryngeal pressure may enlarge the sac. After endoscopic surgery, voice rest is the primary restriction for 1-2 weeks; general aerobic exercise can be resumed within days of uncomplicated endoscopic excision.
dailyManagement: Patients with a conservatively managed laryngocele should monitor daily for new onset of rapidly enlarging neck mass, fever, or sudden breathlessness — signs of laryngopyocele requiring emergency care.
Know the location of the nearest emergency ENT or airway capable service in case acute airway obstruction develops.
Following endoscopic excision, maintain voice rest strictly for the period prescribed (usually 7-14 days) and avoid whispering, which is as straining as speaking loudly.
Return to the surgeon if hoarseness recurs after apparent surgical cure — recurrence or underlying tumour progression requires re-assessment.
Neonates with congenital laryngocele need close respiratory observation by parents and caregivers until definitive surgical correction is performed.
prognosis: The outlook for laryngocele after appropriate surgical management is excellent. Endoscopic excision or marsupialisaton resolves symptoms in 85-95% of internal laryngoceles with a recurrence rate below 5%. External and combined laryngoceles treated by cervical excision also carry high cure rates above 90%. The principal determinant of long-term prognosis is whether an underlying laryngeal malignancy is present: when a laryngocele masks a squamous cell carcinoma, prognosis is governed by tumour stage and oncological treatment. Laryngopyocele carries a mortality risk if airway obstruction is not managed promptly, but with timely intervention, outcomes are excellent. Congenital laryngocele in neonates carries a good prognosis after endoscopic treatment with restoration of normal feeding and breathing. Voice quality after endoscopic surgery is generally preserved; temporary dysphonia of a few weeks is expected.
recoveryTimeline: After endoscopic CO2 laser excision, patients typically stay in hospital for 24-48 hours and return to normal voice use within 2-4 weeks. Professional voice users may need longer vocal rest and laryngological rehabilitation. After external cervical excision, hospitalisation is 2-4 days and full recovery takes 4-6 weeks. Laryngopyocele requires 2-3 weeks of antibiotic treatment and a staged definitive procedure, with full recovery at 6-8 weeks from the initial presentation. Neonates with congenital laryngocele usually breathe and feed normally within days of endoscopic treatment.
complications: Airway obstruction from acute laryngopyocele — the most dangerous complication, requiring immediate emergency airway management and drainage.
Recurrence after surgery — occurs in up to 5% of cases, particularly after incomplete endoscopic marsupialisaton of a large combined laryngocele; requires repeat endoscopy or external revision.
Underlying laryngeal malignancy — present in approximately 15-18% of adult laryngocele cases; delayed diagnosis substantially worsens oncological outcomes.

Dysphonia or voice change after surgery — temporary hoarseness is expected; permanent voice change is uncommon after endoscopic excision but occurs in up to 5% after external operations involving laryngeal framework manipulation.
Wound infection, haematoma, or cervical scar after external excision — occurs in approximately 5-10% of cervical approach cases.

supportResources.name: British Association of Otorhinolaryngologists — Head and Neck Surgeons (ENT UK)
supportResources.description: UK professional body providing patient information on ear, nose, and throat conditions including laryngeal diseases.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Otolaryngology — Head and Neck Surgery (AAO-HNS)
supportResources.description: US ENT professional organisation with patient resources on laryngeal conditions, voice disorders, and finding a head and neck surgeon.

supportResources.name: NHS — Larynx cancer and voice box information
supportResources.description: NHS information on laryngeal pathology, including when to seek urgent review for hoarseness — relevant for patients awaiting laryngocele evaluation.

sources.title: Lancella A et al. Laryngocele: review of the literature and three case reports. Acta Otorhinolaryngol Ital. 2007
Thome R, Thome DC. Endoscopic CO2 laser treatment of laryngocele. Laryngoscope. 2004
Canalis RF et al. Laryngoceles: an updated review. J Otolaryngol. 1977
Dursun G et al. Current diagnosis and treatment of laryngocele in adults. Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg. 2007

confusedWithConditions.name: Laryngeal cancer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Laryngeal cancer presents with progressive hoarseness, a fixed or ulcerated vocal cord, and may secondarily cause a laryngocele by obstructing the saccule — the two can coexist, so malignancy must always be excluded.

confusedWithConditions.name: Epiglottitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Epiglottitis causes rapid-onset severe throat pain, fever, muffled voice, and drooling from inflammatory swelling of the epiglottis, whereas laryngocele is a chronic cystic dilation without fever except when infected.

confusedWithConditions.name: Vocal cord paralysis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Vocal cord paralysis causes hoarseness from immobility of one or both cords, which laryngoscopy shows as a cord fixed in paramedian position — distinct from the submucosal false cord bulge of an internal laryngocele.

relatedConditions.name: Laryngeal cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: An underlying laryngeal carcinoma obstructing the saccule is present in approximately 15-18% of adult laryngocele cases and must be excluded at diagnosis.

relatedConditions.name: Vocal cord paralysis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both conditions cause hoarseness and are diagnosed by laryngoscopy; occasionally co-exist when a laryngeal neoplasm causes both saccular obstruction and vocal cord fixation.

relatedConditions.name: Epiglottitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both can present with acute airway obstruction and are differential diagnoses in the emergency setting of a patient with stridor and a neck mass.

faqs.question: What is a laryngocele?
faqs.answer: A laryngocele is an abnormal air-filled or fluid-filled pouch that forms in the larynx from the expansion of a small natural pocket between the true and false vocal cords called the saccule. It can stay within the larynx, protrude into the neck, or both.

faqs.question: What causes a laryngocele?
faqs.answer: Most adult laryngoceles result from repeatedly raised pressure inside the larynx — from blowing a wind instrument, glass blowing, or chronic coughing — which gradually inflates the saccular pocket. In neonates they are congenital. In about 15-18% of adults a laryngeal tumour blocking the saccule is the cause.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of a laryngocele?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are hoarseness, a soft compressible swelling on the side of the neck that inflates when you blow hard, chronic throat-clearing, and occasionally difficulty breathing. If infected the sac becomes firm and tender and can suddenly obstruct the airway.

faqs.question: Is a laryngocele cancerous?
faqs.answer: Laryngocele itself is not cancerous — it is a benign cystic dilation. However, in about 15-18% of adults a laryngeal cancer is the reason the saccule became blocked and swelled. Every newly found laryngocele should be thoroughly examined by laryngoscopy and biopsy to rule out cancer.

faqs.question: How is a laryngocele diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis starts with flexible laryngoscopy in clinic to see the false cord bulge and check for any mucosal lesion. A CT scan of the neck definitively confirms the type, size, and relationship to surrounding structures and is required before surgery.

faqs.question: How is a laryngocele treated?
faqs.answer: Small asymptomatic laryngoceles can be observed. Symptomatic ones are removed surgically — either by endoscopic laser excision through the mouth or by external surgery through the neck, depending on the type. Infected laryngoceles (laryngopyocele) need antibiotics and urgent drainage.

faqs.question: What is a laryngopyocele?
faqs.answer: A laryngopyocele is a laryngocele that has become infected and filled with pus. The neck mass becomes hard, red, and painful and the airway can become blocked suddenly. It is a medical emergency requiring immediate hospital assessment and surgical drainage.

faqs.question: Can a laryngocele come back after surgery?
faqs.answer: Recurrence occurs in fewer than 5% of cases after complete excision. It is more likely after incomplete endoscopic marsupialisaton of a large combined laryngocele. If hoarseness or a neck swelling returns after surgery, prompt reassessment is needed.

faqs.question: Do wind musicians get laryngoceles?
faqs.answer: Yes. Professional and amateur wind musicians, particularly brass players, are at elevated risk because sustained blowing against resistance repeatedly raises intralaryngeal pressure, gradually inflating the saccular appendix. It is one of the most recognised occupational risk factors.

faqs.question: Will a laryngocele affect my voice permanently?
faqs.answer: Most patients have good or complete voice recovery after endoscopic surgery. Temporary hoarseness of a few weeks is expected. Permanent voice change is uncommon after endoscopic excision and occurs in a small minority after external cervical surgery.

faqs.question: Is laryngocele surgery risky?
faqs.answer: Endoscopic excision is a low-risk procedure with most patients leaving hospital within 24-48 hours. The main risks are temporary hoarseness, recurrence, and rarely airway swelling. External cervical surgery carries small risks of wound infection, haematoma, and voice change in approximately 5-10% of cases.

faqs.question: Can a laryngocele be dangerous?
faqs.answer: A laryngocele becomes dangerous when it gets infected and forms a laryngopyocele, which can suddenly obstruct the airway and is life-threatening without prompt treatment. Any previously stable laryngocele that rapidly enlarges, becomes tender, or causes breathing difficulty needs emergency hospital assessment.

faqs.question: How long is recovery after laryngocele surgery?
faqs.answer: After endoscopic surgery, most patients return to normal activities within 1-2 weeks and voice use within 2-4 weeks. After external cervical surgery, full recovery takes 4-6 weeks. Professional voice users may need additional laryngological rehabilitation before returning to performance.

faqs.question: Can a laryngocele cause breathing difficulties in a baby?
faqs.answer: Yes. Congenital laryngocele in a neonate causes inspiratory stridor and can significantly narrow the airway, leading to respiratory distress and feeding problems. It requires urgent paediatric ENT assessment and early endoscopic treatment to restore normal breathing.

faqs.question: Does a laryngocele need to be removed if it is not causing symptoms?
faqs.answer: Small asymptomatic laryngoceles where cancer has been excluded can sometimes be watched with periodic laryngoscopy and CT. However, because of the risk of infection leading to sudden airway obstruction and the need to rule out underlying malignancy, most specialists recommend removal once a laryngocele is identified in an adult.

faqs.question: What is the difference between an internal and external laryngocele?
faqs.answer: An internal laryngocele stays entirely within the larynx and bulges the false vocal cord inward — visible only on laryngoscopy. An external laryngocele breaks through the thyrohyoid membrane and appears as a palpable neck swelling. Combined types have both components.

faqs.question: Should I tell my anaesthetist I have a laryngocele?
faqs.answer: Yes, always. A laryngocele can make intubation more difficult and the sac can rupture during airway instrumentation. Informing the anaesthetic team beforehand allows them to plan for potential difficult airway management.

faqs.question: Can laryngocele cause sleep apnea?
faqs.answer: A large internal laryngocele can contribute to upper airway narrowing at night, potentially worsening snoring and obstructive sleep apnea. Surgical excision of the laryngocele may partly improve sleep-disordered breathing in these patients, though the primary apnea cause usually requires separate management.

faqs.question: What specialist treats laryngocele?
faqs.answer: An otolaryngologist (ENT surgeon) — ideally with subspecialty laryngology training — is the appropriate specialist. For neonates, a paediatric ENT surgeon is needed. If cancer is found, a head and neck oncology multidisciplinary team takes over management.

metaDescription: A laryngocele is a rare air-filled sac growing from the larynx, causing hoarseness or a neck mass. Learn causes, diagnosis, surgery options and recovery.
